title: Field Service Dispatch and Parts Readiness Modernization
audience_profile: Multi-audience: CFO, operations, and technology leadership
decision_needed: Approve Phase 1 implementation funding and authorize a controlled pilot before enterprise rollout.
decision_owner: VP of Operations, with CFO funding approval
requested_action: Approve a $690,000 Year 1 investment to implement dispatch optimization, parts readiness controls, and governance reporting across two pilot regions.
decision_timing: Decision requested by 2026-06-30 to begin pilot mobilization in July.
consequence_of_delay: A delay past June 30 defers pilot benefits by at least one quarter and preserves an estimated $190,000 per month in avoidable repeat visits, overtime, and expedited parts costs.
problem_statement: Field service teams are losing dispatch productivity and customer appointment reliability because technician routing, parts readiness, and exception escalation are managed across disconnected tools and inconsistent local practices.
observable_symptoms: First-time fix rate is estimated at 71% against an internal target of 82%.
Technicians report arriving without required parts on roughly 14% of service visits.
Dispatch supervisors manually rebalance schedules late in the day three to four times per week.
root_cause_hypothesis: The working hypothesis is that the issue is not technician capacity alone; it is fragmented dispatch planning and weak parts-readiness governance before the appointment is committed.
root_cause_challenge: The common diagnosis is that the organization needs more technicians. The evidence suggests capacity pressure is real, but avoidable repeat visits and late schedule changes are consuming capacity that should first be recovered through process, data, and governance improvements.
status_quo_impact: $2.3M estimated annual cost of inaction, based on $1.15M repeat-visit labor cost, $620K overtime and expedited parts cost, and $530K revenue leakage from missed same-week appointments.
urgency_driver: Peak service season begins in Q4. If the pilot does not start in July, the organization will enter peak season without validated routing and parts-readiness controls.

strategic_alignment.connection: Reduces missed service windows and repeat visits.
Targets overtime, truck rolls, expedited parts, and avoidable rework.
Creates common readiness gates and escalation paths across regions.

current_state: Dispatch, inventory, and work-order readiness data are reviewed in separate systems. Supervisors reconcile exceptions manually, and there is no consistent pre-appointment gate confirming technician skill, part availability, and customer readiness before a visit is locked.

baseline_metrics.metric: Average repeat truck rolls per month

gap_analysis: The organization has local heroics but not a repeatable readiness model. Dispatch decisions are optimized too late, parts gaps are discovered after commitments are made, and escalation ownership is unclear when a service order is at risk.
evidence_limitations: Baseline figures are fictional demonstration estimates. In a real case, they should be validated against work-order history, inventory availability, technician time logs, and customer appointment data.
desired_future_state: A governed dispatch and parts readiness process that confirms service order readiness before commitment, flags at-risk appointments early, and gives operations leadership weekly visibility into exceptions, first-time fix rate, and capacity leakage.
scope_out: National rollout before pilot exit criteria

evaluation_criteria.rationale: Pilot must begin before peak season.
Field teams cannot absorb a heavy process redesign during active service periods.
Funding approval depends on credible cost avoidance.
Pilot design should extend to additional regions if validated.
Integration risk must remain bounded in Phase 1.

options.name: Option 0: Do Nothing
options.description: Maintain current regional dispatch and parts-readiness practices.
options.cost: $0 new investment; estimated $2.3M annual cost of inaction remains
options.rationale: Lowest disruption but preserves avoidable cost and service reliability risk.

options.name: Option 1: Add technician capacity
options.description: Hire or contract additional field technicians to absorb repeat visits and peak-season demand.
options.timeline: 4-6 months to recruit, onboard, and route effectively
options.rationale: Treats visible capacity pressure but not the process leakage consuming capacity.

options.name: Option 2: Recommended - Dispatch and parts readiness pilot
options.description: Implement a two-region pilot with readiness gates, exception queue, dispatch optimization rules, weekly operating dashboard, and defined escalation ownership.
benefits: Targets root cause of capacity leakage
tradeoffs: Requires light integration and data cleanup
options.rationale: Best risk-adjusted path because it addresses avoidable work before adding permanent capacity.

options.name: Option 3: Full platform replacement
options.description: Replace dispatch, inventory readiness, and field scheduling stack with a fully integrated field service platform.
tradeoffs: Too slow for current peak-season issue
options.rationale: May become a future option, but too large and slow for the current decision window.

recommended_solution: Approve Option 2: a two-region dispatch and parts readiness pilot with defined readiness gates, exception ownership, and operating metrics before any national rollout.
rationale: Option 2 addresses the likely root cause while preserving reversibility. It is less expensive and faster than platform replacement, and it avoids adding permanent labor before recovering avoidable capacity leakage.
solution_people: Regional service directors sponsor the pilot; dispatch supervisors own daily exception review; inventory planning owns parts readiness; IT supports data extracts and dashboard enablement.
solution_process: Before appointments are locked, orders are checked for technician skill, parts availability, customer readiness, and schedule feasibility. Exceptions enter a supervisor-owned queue with escalation thresholds.
solution_technology: Use current work-order, inventory, and scheduling systems with a lightweight reporting layer and exception workflow for the pilot. Avoid major platform replacement in Phase 1.
organizational_impact: The pilot changes how supervisors commit appointments and manage exceptions. It adds up-front readiness discipline but should reduce late-day rework, repeat visits, and overtime pressure.

costs.assumption: Internal project, operations, and IT labor valued at loaded cost for pilot setup.
Vendor support for dashboard, exception workflow, and field reporting.
Supervisor and technician training materials, office hours, and adoption support.
Lightweight reporting layer, data extracts, and support.

benefits.name: Reduced repeat visits
benefits.basis: Assumes pilot practices reduce repeat truck rolls by 40% in scaled steady state.

benefits.name: Reduced overtime and expedited parts
benefits.basis: Assumes fewer late schedule changes and fewer emergency parts movements.

benefits.name: Recovered appointment capacity
benefits.basis: Assumes recovered field capacity supports additional same-week appointments without adding headcount.

benefits.name: Supervisor operating visibility
benefits.basis: Improves decision quality and escalation discipline.

benefits.name: Customer experience improvement
benefits.basis: Expected improvement from fewer missed or repeated service windows.

financial_summary.npv: Not calculated in sample; add discount rate for CFO-ready version

assumptions.assumption: Repeat-visit reduction reaches 40% of addressable repeat truck rolls
assumptions.validation: Validate during 90-day pilot against baseline regions.

assumptions.assumption: Data extracts from current systems are sufficient for pilot reporting
assumptions.validation: Confirm in Phase 0 technical readiness review.

assumptions.assumption: Two pilot regions are representative enough for scale decision
assumptions.validation: Compare route density, parts mix, and technician skill distribution before selection.

sensitivity_scenarios.impact: Payback approximately 4.1 months after scaled run-rate.
First-year benefit deferred by roughly one month; scale decision slips into Q4.

risks.risk: Supervisor adoption risk: supervisors may bypass readiness gates under daily schedule pressure.
risks.mitigation: Use daily exception review, pilot office hours, and VP Operations reinforcement during the first 60 days.

risks.risk: Data quality risk: inventory and work-order data may not be reliable enough for automated readiness flags.
risks.mitigation: Run Phase 0 data profiling and manual validation before automated flags are used for decisions.

risks.risk: Pilot representativeness risk: selected regions may not generalize to national rollout.
risks.mitigation: Select regions with different route density and parts mix; require scale-decision evidence by segment.

risks.risk: Change load risk: technicians may perceive readiness checks as administrative overhead.
risks.mitigation: Limit technician-facing changes; route most readiness work through dispatch and supervisor workflows.

constraints.constraint: No full platform replacement in Phase 1
constraints.impact: Pilot must use existing systems and lightweight workflow/reporting.

constraints.constraint: Pilot must not disrupt peak-season readiness
constraints.impact: Implementation must complete before Q4 freeze.

implementation_milestones.deliverables: Baseline metrics, data quality report, pilot region selection
Exception queue, readiness dashboard, reporting cadence
Daily adoption review, weekly metrics, issue log
Pilot results, financial validation, rollout recommendation

quick_wins: At-risk appointments visible before daily dispatch lock
Weekly repeat-visit trend by cause code
governance: A weekly pilot operating review will track adoption, repeat visits, parts-not-ready exceptions, overtime, and customer appointment reliability. A steering review at the end of Phase 2 will decide whether to scale, revise, or stop.
escalation_path: Pilot blockers unresolved within two business days escalate from Operations PM to Regional Service Director; unresolved cross-functional blockers escalate to VP Operations in the weekly steering review.

review_gates.criteria: Baseline confirmed; data access feasible; pilot regions selected
Adoption above 80%; data quality defects below threshold; early trend positive
Validated financial trend, adoption, and operational feasibility

open_questions: Confirm exact loaded technician labor rate
Confirm whether current tools can support exception workflow without custom development
